metadata.title: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
metadata.color_semantics: claude: INK #3D3929 all text; SPARK #D97757 the one accent. Content: TEAL = quantum / transmitted; CRIMSON = classical / blocked / exponential decay
metadata.greeting_note: hello lexicon: French. Medhavy 1-word budget. Wagwan is Bear-only.
metadata.note: Refactored to Claude cut, claude-medhavy channel (claude-refactor skill). B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk cold open (Wonder register, new audio). ASK micro-beats inserted before each Manim/GRAPHIC beat (B01a, B03a, B04a, B05a, B06a, B07a, B08a). B11 = HANDOFF, B12 = ClaudeTitleOutro replacing OutroSeries/OutroCTA. Inner beats B02–B10 narration and media untouched.
metadata.audio_note: All existing inner-beat mp3s were generated with ElevenLabs NBB voice. Must regenerate ALL with kokoro af_kore for Medhavy: python3 runtime/scripts/generate_audio.py quantum-mechanics-vol1/youtube/vox-quantum-tunneling

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Classically a particle with less energy than a barrier always reflects, never crossing it. The wavefunction does not stop at the wall; it decays exponentially inside the barrier instead of dying instantly. If the barrier is thin, a small amplitude survives to the far edge and launches a transmitted wave. Transmission falls off like e to the minus two kappa L, with no borrowed energy, just what waves do.

artifactLines: Classically a particle with less energy than a barrier always reflects, never crossing it.
The wavefunction does not stop at the wall; it decays exponentially inside the barrier instead of dying instantly.
If the barrier is thin, a small amplitude survives to the far edge and launches a transmitted wave.
Transmission falls off like e to the minus two kappa L, with no borrowed energy, just what waves do.

beats.narration_text: So the transmitted amplitude is just the exponential tail e to the minus two kappa L surviving to the far edge, no borrowed energy at all. I want to apply it to alpha decay. Claude, please (1) explain how an alpha particle trapped in a nucleus escapes by tunneling through the Coulomb barrier; (2) show why a tiny change in barrier width or energy swings the half-life across many orders of magnitude; (3) relate that sensitivity to the same exponential factor in the 5 eV, 5 angstrom electron example.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: So the transmitted amplitude is just the exponential tail e to the minus two kappa L surviving to the far edge, no borrowed energy at all. I want to apply it to alpha decay. Claude, please (1) explain how an alpha particle trapped in a nucleus escapes by tunneling through the Coulomb barrier; (2) show why a tiny change in barrier width or energy swings the half-life across many orders of magnitude; (3) relate that sensitivity to the same exponential factor in the 5 eV, 5 angstrom electron example.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: classical particle with energy E less than barrier height V0 bouncing back — total reflection, no tunneling"

beats.narration_text: Classical rule: if your energy is less than the barrier height, you bounce. Always. Every single time. That's not a quantum thing — that's just energy conservation.
Quantum particle, same barrier, same energy — less than the barrier height. Classical prediction: total reflection. But some fraction gets through. Why?

card.copy: Classical physics predicts total reflection when E < V. Here some fraction transmits anyway. Why?

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: quantum wavefunction entering a barrier and decaying exponentially inside — evanescent tail persisting through the barrier width"

beats.narration_text: The key: the wavefunction doesn't stop at the wall. Where the barrier begins, it doesn't hit a cliff and die — it starts decaying, exponentially, into the barrier.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: quantum tunneling — exponential decay inside the barrier, small but nonzero amplitude surviving to the far edge, launching a transmitted wave"

beats.narration_text: Exponential decay never reaches exactly zero. If the barrier is thin enough, a small but non-zero amplitude survives to the far edge — and that amplitude launches a transmitted wave.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: transmission probability T proportional to exp(-2κL) vs barrier width L, showing exponential decay — also plot vs barrier height V0"

beats.narration_text: The transmitted amplitude falls off like e to the minus two kappa L — where kappa captures how classically forbidden the region is, and L is the barrier width. Thicker barrier, exponentially less transmission.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: tunneling particle arriving on the far side with the same energy it started with — annotate that energy is conserved, no energy was borrowed"

beats.narration_text: No energy was borrowed. The particle that arrives on the far side has the same energy it had before — it tunneled through the classically forbidden region, not over it.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: real-world tunneling — alpha decay from a nucleus, proton-proton fusion in the Sun, STM tip sensing atoms — show the classically forbidden barrier in each case"

beats.narration_text: Tunneling is real and measurable. Alpha decay, nuclear fusion in the Sun, scanning tunneling microscopes — all run on this exponential tail surviving through classically forbidden regions.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: claude "write a Manim scene: worked example — electron with 1 eV hitting 5 eV barrier 5 angstroms wide, calculate kappa, show exponential decay, label transmission probability roughly 10^-4"

beats.narration_text: Illustrative example: an electron with 1 eV hits a 5 eV barrier, 5 angstroms wide. The transmission is about one in ten thousand — tiny, but exactly what the exponential formula predicts.
The wavefunction leaks because exponentials never hit zero. That leak is tunneling — not magic, not borrowed energy, just what waves do inside classically forbidden regions.

card.copy: Exponentials never reach zero. The leak is the tunnel.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Three barrier widths. Ask Claude to calculate the transmission probability for each and plot how it falls. See the exponential drop with your own numbers.

props.segment: Why a Particle Can Walk Through a Wall
props.command: model electron tunneling through a 5 eV barrier of width 1, 2, and 5 angstroms — plot transmission probability vs barrier width for an electron with 1 eV kinetic energy
